The role.

You can tell me if that's different than Jason's because I, we haven't really talked about it in a little while.
And then we can talk about you.
but I lead the like sort of delivery side of the organization, just in terms of like process, people, all that stuff, operations.

And one of the things that we find as we're, as we're building and delivering content is I think the teams get a little bit too like focused on the, on the trees and like can't take a step back at a 30,000 foot view.
And like, um,
And think about like shifting strategies, SEO analysis, like a higher level of just like SEO, geo experience and strategy.
They just, when you're in the day to day of like delivery, delivery, delivery, we're doing five articles a week per client, you're managing multiple clients, you just can't take that step back.

And so we've started thinking about having somebody who can almost operate like a strike force where you're stepping in, you're like, okay, we're about to do a QBR, or we've been doing the same strategy for two to three months.
Like, how are things working, kind of keeping an eye of like the broad patterns and indicators that show kind of where we should be going in terms of strategy.
Like some of that's just experience on the team.
Like some people have like really deep, like editorial experience, some people, and just some people are less experienced.
So I think that like my perspective is less like technically this is what we need and more like operationally this is how this person would work.
Is that, does that helpful?
Is that helpful?
Does that make sense?
That makes sense and that's the exact context I have from Jason as well, so.
Okay, perfect.
Awesome.
Yeah, so like would love to like learn a little bit about you.
You're kind of where you fit in and all of this and yeah, let's go.
sure thing.
So my background is in engineering, but I've never really held an engineering job in my life.
Right out of college, I started working with a company called GSM Nation.
They used to sell unlocked smartphones in the US before they were a thing.
And I did content and SEO for them for a couple of years until they were acquired.
Uh.
That's
Then I moved to pro-Pakistani, which was essentially the tech crunch of Pakistan at that time.
I was the first employee there, stayed there for six years, and took it from about 1 to 45 employees, went to 17 million pageviews per month, and grew their revenue by 11x.
And then we had a majority share acquisition of the company, and that's the point where I left that company and moved to the U.S.
to be with my wife.
When I moved to the U.S., it was beat COVID, and my initial plan was that I was going to do an MBA because that would help bridge the gap from Pakistan to the U.S.
and sort of like acclimate me to how things work here.
But then I realized that I did not want to drop 250K on a Zoom degree, so I ended up joining OnDeck in their first 50K program, which was designed to help people break into startups.
So that gave me a lot of clarity, and I worked with a lot of smart people, and I just completely dropped the MBA plan after that, and I joined Optimist, which is an agency.
As well, that has worked with companies like Superhuman, Glide, ZoomInfo, and a few others.
So I worked with them for a few months, and then ended up joining OnDeck full-time, and I led growth for the founder-focused programs.
So that was ODF, and we also launched ODX, which was a YC competitor at that time.
I think we got like 350 investments and about 12,000 applications in six months or so.
But then our CDCP financing from Tybico Global fell out, and the company sort of dwindled.
After that, I joined Stable, which is a virtual mailbox for businesses.
I was the first person on the marketing team there, and I stayed there for about a year or so.
And in that time, we doubled revenue.
I set up the self-serve motion.
We stress-tested the sales-led motion as well, and that's the one they actually ended up going with to grow about like 5 to 10x since I left.
I was responsible for the self-serve side of the business, and they just like decided to deprioritize.
So that's when I left that company.
Over the past couple of years, I've been consulting.
It's been, initially, it was for some of the work was product marketing, but I work with companies like Spot Draft, which is a series B legal type company that competes with DocuSign.
I did some growth marketing stuff for companies like Ascend, which is a fundraising management platform that works with NFL teams and a few others.
But over the past year or so, I've been focusing a lot more on content and SEO.
I say that I'm a content consultant, but at the back end, I've sort of launched a mini agency of my own.
So I have like a couple of people I work with.
I have about like five clients so far.
Some of them are like enterprise level.
So that would be Click.
Some of them are much smaller.
So HostFamily, which is a vacation rental management platform and FindHR, which is a software aggregator.
So I've run the gamut of like working across growth, across product marketing, and now content and SEO.
And I've got, like, over 15 years of experience of doing this.
So, yeah, that's the context.
Amazing.
That sounds great.

Can you talk to me about communicating SEO strategy to non-technical clients or even clients just, we have a lot of clients where they have experience in one side of marketing, but, like, maybe not, like, growth in SEO.
How do you help, like, express priorities and communicate, like, strategy to them?
Like, what's important?
So I like connecting stuff to business goals.
So I think, like, that's, like, the most fundamental requirement to communicate to any executive audience.

And that's something I feel like gets sort of lost in translation because teams tend to focus on, we grew our clicks by this much, we grew our impressions by this much, but business teams and, like, executive teams don't really care about that.
They want to know what their actual revenue impact of that was.
So I think, like, first understanding what is the goal that the person on the other side of the table is actually trying to improve upon.
Like, what is their OKR?
And how does that ladder up to R work with them?
So just understanding that equation, I feel like that's, like, the most fundamentally important part of communication with any executive team.
And I don't like to throw, like, every single thing that I'm doing to the client, because that might not be important.
So I just try to focus on, like, what are the most key metrics that are important to them and their job?
Essentially, what makes them look good if it goes up?
And sort of, like, communicate that.
And if they want more detail in terms of what are we doing on a tactical basis?
What backlinks are we building?
What content are we doing?
What programmatic SEO players are we running?
How is the performance for that trending, et cetera?
That can, like, be an additional level of detail that we can go into, but it's not necessarily.
Going to be present in the deck, in the V1, essentially.
Yeah.
How do you document, like, process and decisions to others?
So if it's small, I'm probably just going to make a Loom video and share that.
But I am a pretty big fan of just documenting every single, so any single thing that is repeatable, I try to make a Google Doc for it.
And I add, like, screenshots and screencasts out of everything that I'm doing.
So it just depends on the workflow itself and the people and what their preferences are in terms of consumption of that said workflow.
But for me, ideally speaking, it usually looks like a very detailed document that has screenshots, that has video recordings.
And ideally, the person doesn't need to come to me to get any clarification.
It should be self-contained in that way.
So that's, like, my ideal way of operating.
Yeah, okay.
And then can you...
Can talk to me about a time when you've had conflicting requests from clients and how you think about prioritizing across?
Yeah, so I was literally on a discovery call with somebody, and they were super, super focused on, hey, I want the rankings for this particular keyword group to go up.
And I really had to push back on why they wanted to do that in the first place.
So I was like, hey, you tell me that the keyword rankings for this should go up.
And that makes sense to me.
I can help you do that.
But what are you actually trying to achieve here?
Like, what is the revenue goal that you want to hit for Q4?
What is that number?
And how does SEO or AEU or whatever, how does that ladder up to that particular number?
So do you expect SEO to drive 20% off the quarterly goal?
If yes, then, of course, we can help that keyword cluster move up in rankings, but there might be other opportunities that are missing as well.
So whenever.
It sort of got through to them, and they were like super adamant on just like, I want the keyword rankings for this to go up and nothing else.
Yeah, that's awesome.
Can you give me an example of a time that you've adapted quickly to changing client needs?
Yeah, so I have this one client where we had an initial three-month engagement, and the scope was that they're an aggregator site, much like G2 or Keptera.
And the initial goal was that we would do a programmatic SEO strategy, we would publish about 18 pages per month.
But we've done that for about two months or so, but the pages aren't cranking quickly enough, and the results simply aren't there yet.
So I talked to them about increasing the scope, and they already have an in-house team.
So I just talked to them very transparently that, hey, we can continue doing this, what we've been doing so far.
But candidly, this is not going to drive the number of leads that we want to in order to...
To this engagement like a net positive for you.
So let's brainstorm and figure out what we can do together.
So we cut down the scope in terms of how many programmatic page templates we were going for.
We were going to go for three.
We just decided to do two because they were already done.
And then I enlisted some help from the internal teams to scale production massively.
So I created a workflow of essentially how we populate those pages.
handed it off to their team.
And now we're going to be publishing about 100 pages per month.
So we changed the scope.
And this literally happened two weeks ago.
So this is super fresh in my mind.
Yeah.
I mean, actually, that's like a good segue question.

And the last thing that I have to ask you, which is thinking about like that short term versus long term, because like you could have kept going on the strategy and like maybe in six to nine months, it might have paid off.
So how do you balance the two of like short versus long term?
Um, I think I feel like, uh, there are certain goals that any company.
If they don't do that, then they're essentially dead.
So you need to understand what that number is, and that's essentially the minimum thing you need to commit to and deliver on no matter what happens.

But beyond that, I feel like, and I try to push for this as much as possible, about 10 to 20% of the capacity, even if you're super focused on the particular quarter, should be focused on essentially stating the ship in the right direction.
So you don't end up crashing into an iceberg, even though your velocity was super fast.
So it kind of depends on the particular client and their appetite for essentially shifting that balance.
But it just like the core crux is that you need to understand what are the minimum goals we need to hit.
And once you've actually hit that, then you have some bandwidth or headroom to sort of figure out where we're going in the, are we going in the right direction?
Are we doing the right things?
Does the current thing we do allow us to, for example, does
Grow for the next six quarters or so.
Is it going to fall flat?
This tactic is going to run out of steam in like two quarters.
Will we have to essentially go back to the drawing board in two quarters or not?
So you do need like sort of that executive function at the top, sort of figure out like, if I'm the captain of this ship, am I going in the right direction?
But at the same time, you do need the engine to be working all the time as well.

So it's like a very delicate balancing act, but essentially like figure out what are the minimum goals for the three months for the particular quarter, and then sort of zoom out and see like, where are we going in Q2, Q3, Q4, etc.
Yeah, great.
Cool.
Well, those are all the questions that I have for you.
What questions do you have for me?
Yeah.

Who the teams would be?
Yeah.

I think both of us are thinking about this as not, like, you wouldn't be serving any specific client over a long period of time.
It would really be, so, like, we have, like, smaller startup clients and very similar to your agency.
Like, smaller startup clients, larger enterprise clients.

Um.
Um.
As we start to, like, go into year two fully of these, of this company.

And it would really be the whole, like, book of business.
I don't, like, that being said, I could see this being, like, a team of SEO specialists.
But we kind of want to see, like, how this works and, and what this layer looks like.

Would I essentially have a blank slate and sort of figure out what I want to do?

So, yeah, there's no, like, we don't want to.

can jump into anything else that might be on your mind.
